review_summary: Plan is well-structured and covers both sections correctly. Three must-fix issues found: (1) the continueOnBlock exit-code protocol is contradicted within the plan itself — Task 3 notes say exit 0 but the Context section says denyExit/exit 2; (2) hooks.json structure places continueOnBlock at the wrong nesting level per the actual file schema; (3) the `output()` type union in hook-output.ts needs to be extended to accept BlockOutput (already exists as a type but output() already accepts it — actually a non-issue on closer inspection). One should-fix on the execSync/PATH assumption for create(), and one suggestion on inline types.

issues.title: continueOnBlock exit-code protocol is self-contradictory in the plan
issues.description: Task 3 'Key Decisions / Notes' states: 'The hook still exits normally (not exit 2) — output(continueOnBlock(reason)) then process.exit(0).' But the Context section (line 77) states: 'The hook's denyExit still exits 2 — only the hooks.json field controls whether CC terminates or feeds it back.' These are mutually contradictory. The actual hook-output.ts shows that output() writes JSON to stdout and does NOT call process.exit — the caller (main()) is responsible for exit code. CC's continueOnBlock behaviour is triggered by the hooks.json flag regardless of exit code; however, a hook that emits { decision: 'block' } and then exits 0 will be treated as a hint-level advisory by CC, NOT as a block at all — decision:'block' only takes effect when paired with exit code 2. If the intent is 'soft block that feeds back as context', the correct protocol is: emit { decision:'block', reason } to stdout AND write reason to stderr AND exit 2 (same as denyExit, but with continueOnBlock:true in hooks.json telling CC to re-feed rather than terminate). Exiting 0 with { decision:'block' } will silently downgrade to no-op or advisory. The plan must pick one protocol and document it consistently: the correct choice is exit 2 + continueOnBlock:true in hooks.json.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove the 'process.exit(0)' instruction from Task 3. Instead, add a new helper to hook-output.ts: `export function blockExit(reason: string): never { process.stderr.write(reason); process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({ decision: 'block', reason })); process.exit(2); }`. Update file-checker.ts main() to call `blockExit(result)` instead of `output(continueOnBlock(result))`. The continueOnBlock:true in hooks.json is what prevents CC from terminating the turn — the exit code must still be 2 for CC to recognise it as a block at all. Delete the no-exit-2 note from both the Context section and Task 3.

issues.title: hooks.json continueOnBlock is at the wrong nesting level per the actual file schema
issues.description: The plan's Task 3 shows the continueOnBlock field inside the hooks array item (the inner object with 'type', 'command', 'args', 'timeout'). Reading the actual hooks.json (confirmed via Read), the file structure is: outer object { matcher?, if?, hooks: [ { type, command, args, timeout, async? } ] }. The inner hooks array items are the command descriptors. CC's continueOnBlock field belongs on the OUTER matcher/event object, not on the individual command descriptor inside hooks[]. If placed on the inner item it will be silently ignored. Compare how 'async: true' is placed on the inner item — that IS correctly on the inner item per CC docs — but continueOnBlock is a different field that belongs at the outer hooks-entry level. The plan's JSON example is therefore wrong.
issues.suggested_fix: Change the hooks.json modification to add 'continueOnBlock': true to the OUTER object containing the file-checker entry, i.e. at the same level as 'matcher' and 'if', not inside the hooks array item. The corrected outer object should be: { "matcher": "Write|Edit|MultiEdit", "if": "Write(*.ts)|...", "continueOnBlock": true, "hooks": [ { "type": "command", "command": "sentinal", "args": ["hook", "claude", "file-checker"], "timeout": 10 } ] }. Verify this against the CC 2.1.139 changelog entry for continueOnBlock to confirm the nesting.

issues.title: Task 1 create() via execSync requires sentinal to be in PATH — untestable and fragile in plugin context
issues.description: Task 1 documents two options for create(): (a) call a sidecar worktree/create route (acknowledged as not existing yet), or (b) use Node's execSync to call the installed sentinal binary. Option (b) requires sentinal to be in PATH at OpenCode plugin load time. The OpenCode plugin runs in the OpenCode process, whose PATH may differ from the user's shell PATH (especially on macOS where GUI apps don't inherit shell PATH). Additionally, the testing strategy says 'use spies to avoid real git/sidecar calls' — but if create() calls execSync('sentinal worktree create ...'), the test must either spy on execSync (which is possible but fragile) or the code must accept an injectable executor. More critically: the plan's DoD for Task 1 only checks that create() 'tested with mocked sidecar' — there is no sidecar worktree/create route to mock, so this DoD criterion is actually testing the wrong path. The plan's Context section also says 'Worktree create is NOT a sidecar route — use WorktreeManager.create() directly or spawn sentinal via $ shell helper' but then correctly notes WorktreeManager uses Bun.spawnSync (not safe in Node context). The plan ends up with execSync as the answer but doesn't address the PATH or testability concerns in the DoD.
issues.suggested_fix: Either: (A) Add a POST /worktree/create sidecar route (1-2 hour addition to src/sidecar/worktree-routes.ts) so create() delegates to the sidecar which runs in Bun and can safely call WorktreeManager — this also makes the test straightforward (spy on sidecar client). Or (B) keep execSync but require the implementer to inject the executor as a dependency: `createSpecWorktreeAdaptor(sidecar, executor = (cmd) => execSync(cmd))` so tests can inject a no-op spy without spawning real git. Document the PATH requirement explicitly in the Risks table and add a runtime check that logs a clear error if `sentinal` is not found. Update the Task 1 DoD to specifically cover the 'sentinal not in PATH' error path.

issues.title: WorkspaceAdaptor type import strategy is unresolved and could break the build
issues.description: Task 1 notes two options for importing WorkspaceAdaptor: (a) import from '@opencode-ai/plugin' (installed at ~/.config/opencode/node_modules/), or (b) inline the types as a local interface. The plan says 'check how other imports work' and 'Alternatively, inline the types' — this is unresolved and left to the implementer. The critical issue: @opencode-ai/plugin is installed in the OpenCode global config directory (~/.config/opencode/node_modules/), NOT in the sentinal project's node_modules. A direct `import type { WorkspaceAdaptor } from '@opencode-ai/plugin'` in src/opencode/workspace-adaptor.ts will fail tsc (cannot resolve module) because the package is not in sentinal's package.json or bun.lock. Task 2 correctly notes 'do NOT import from @opencode-ai/plugin in the plugin file itself' but Task 1 doesn't make this constraint explicit for the new workspace-adaptor.ts module.
issues.suggested_fix: Explicitly mandate inline types in Task 1: define `WorkspaceAdaptor`, `WorkspaceInfo`, and `WorkspaceTarget` as local interfaces in `src/opencode/workspace-adaptor.ts` (copy-paste the ~10 lines from the Context section of the plan into the file). Do NOT add @opencode-ai/plugin to sentinal's package.json — it's a peer dep supplied by the OpenCode runtime. Add this as a hard constraint in Task 1's Key Decisions: 'Do not import from @opencode-ai/plugin — define all workspace types inline.' Update the Task 4 DoD to include `bunx tsc --noEmit` passing with no module resolution errors.

issues.title: Task 3 test update scope is incomplete — file-checker.test.ts assertion change not specified precisely
issues.description: Task 3 notes 'file-checker.test.ts currently asserts on hint output shape — update to assert { decision: "block", reason: ... }'. Reading the actual file-checker.ts, the current main() calls output(hint('PostToolUse', result)) when violations found. The test file is not read here, but the change from HintOutput shape to BlockOutput shape is a breaking change to the test assertions. The plan documents this change is needed but does not specify whether the test should also verify the exit code (exit 2 vs exit 0) — which is directly tied to the must_fix above about the exit code protocol. If the exit code issue is fixed (exit 2 via blockExit), the tests need to handle process.exit(2) being called, which requires either mocking process.exit or testing via the exported processFileCheck() function only (not main()). The plan's DoD only says 'All tests pass' without clarifying how process.exit(2) is handled in tests.
issues.suggested_fix: Update Task 3 testing notes to specify: tests should call processFileCheck() directly (not main()) to avoid the process.exit(2) issue in tests — this is consistent with the existing test pattern described in sentinal-testing.md ('test them by feeding a HookInput through the exported function directly'). main() wiring (blockExit call) is verified by the build succeeding. Add an explicit note: 'Do not test main() end-to-end in unit tests — only test processFileCheck() return values and hook-output.ts helper shapes.'

issues.title: Task 2 Wave 2 parallel with Task 3 is safe — no file overlap, no issue
issues.description: The review was asked to check for file overlap between Task 2 (targets/opencode/plugins/sentinal.ts) and Task 3 (src/utils/hook-output.ts, src/hooks/file-checker.ts, targets/claude-code/hooks/hooks.json). These are entirely disjoint file sets. No overlap issue exists. Task 2 and Task 3 can safely run concurrently in Wave 2 as designed.
issues.suggested_fix: No action needed. This is a confirmation that the wave design is correct.
